title: Itchy Amp Scratchy Land

jokes.text: The violentest place on Earth
jokes.setup: Standard superlative describing a theme park
jokes.punchline: Made-up word 'violentest' instead of 'most violent'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty-ism - sounds like marketing speak but with his characteristic grammatical incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Welcome to the violentest theme park on Earth!

jokes.text: Look at this Bible I just got, 15 bucks. And talk about a preachy book. Everybody's a sinner, except for this guy.
jokes.punchline: Calling the Bible 'preachy' with complete obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - manages to critique the Bible as 'preachy' while completely missing the point of religion. Perfect character stupidity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Everything's too expensive these days! Food, gas, rent... even the Bible costs money!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And another thing - I tried reading it once. Way too preachy!

jokes.text: We're going to the Highway 9 bird sanctuary. I understand they've installed a new bird feeder this year. It's shaped like a diner. And it's on this really tall pole.
jokes.punchline: Absurdly mundane details about a bird feeder as if it's exciting
jokes.explanation: Pure Marge - finding excitement in the most boring possible vacation while being completely sincere

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, instead of going to the beach, we could stay home and watch the birds at our new feeder!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Birds? Marge, that sounds boring.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh Homer, it's fascinating! Just yesterday, a cardinal landed on the perch for a full seven seconds. And the way the sunlight catches the seeds in the dispenser? Simply magnificent. Plus, we can keep a journal of which birds visit and at what time. The sparrows usually come around 2 PM, and the finches prefer the nyjer seed. It's like having nature's own little television show, right here in our backyard!

jokes.text: Mom, Dad, Bart's dead! That's right. Dead serious about going to Itchy & Scratchy Land.
jokes.punchline: Reveal it's just wordplay about being serious
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection gag, executed competently but not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, I have some serious news.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no! What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm dead serious.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're dead?!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No, man. Dead serious. Like, I'm really serious about something.

jokes.text: over 100 bars and saloons... and a world-class chemical-dependency center
jokes.punchline: Casually including rehab facility as if it's a selling point
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - the park acknowledges its bars will create alcoholics and conveniently provides treatment

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You've got a world-class spa, a championship golf course, five-star restaurants, and a state-of-the-art rehab facility!

jokes.text: Recipe-related bumper cars
jokes.punchline: Completely nonsensical theme for bumper cars
jokes.explanation: Pure absurdist humor - the randomness of connecting recipes to bumper cars is inexplicably funny

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Look, they have bumper cars themed to 'The history of cuticles.'

jokes.text: Hey, look, Marge. They're still not fighting back. I can be a jerk and no one can stop me.
jokes.punchline: Homer explicitly acknowledging and enjoying being terrible to pacifists
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer - completely missing the moral lesson and instead finding the worst possible takeaway

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, being mean to pacifists is easy! And it's fun! I'm gonna do this every day!

jokes.text: Suckers. Sharkboy!
jokes.setup: Homer calling beach-goers suckers for paying
jokes.punchline: Immediately gets attacked by shark, proving he's the sucker
jokes.explanation: Perfect instant karma setup-payoff. Homer's hubris immediately punished in the most literal way possible.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're all suckers for paying to go to the beach!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Immediately, a shark attacked Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Lobster hat, fishnet Speedo Jr., wheelie shoes... invisible-dog leash. Well, I'm packed.
jokes.punchline: Completely ridiculous collection of useless novelty items
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's terrible judgment and love of useless gadgets

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let me pack for our vacation. I'll need my inflatable neck pillow shaped like a lobster, my collection of commemorative spoons from places I've never been, my pet rock with googly eyes, my kazoo that only plays in B-flat, and my life-size cardboard cutout of myself.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why would you bring any of that?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Because Marge, a man without useless novelty items is no man at all.

jokes.text: No. I ain't senile, dad blast it... Grampa's Little Helper? What's that? Which one of you is the mailman?
jokes.punchline: Immediately demonstrates senility by misremembering the dog's name and not recognizing family
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa Simpson moment - the contradiction between his claim and immediate evidence is flawless

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm not senile! My mind is sharp as a tack!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Why, just the other day I was playing fetch with... uh... Rovio?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, that's Santa's Little Helper.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Santa? What's Santa doing in our house?

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Grampa, it's your grandson Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Homer? I don't know any Homer. Where's my son?

jokes.text: Don't worry. I've got an ace up my sleeve.
jokes.setup: Homer's confident declaration on the road
jokes.punchline: Implied visual of Homer having literally nothing or something absurd
jokes.explanation: Setup for visual gag that relies on Homer's overconfidence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not just driving aimlessly. I have a destination in mind.

jokes.text: What's the matter with you? We don't have any fruits or vegetables in the car. The whole trunk's full of them, Marge.
jokes.setup: Homer confidently denying they have produce
jokes.punchline: Marge reveals the trunk is full of exactly what he denied
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness - completely wrong while being confidently condescending

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We don't have any produce in this car!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, open the trunk.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fine, but you'll see there's nothing—

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: *opens trunk* Homer, it's completely full of produce.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Sign of evil number four... Continuing our sign-of-evil countdown, here's Vanessa Williams.
jokes.setup: Religious radio about signs of evil
jokes.punchline: Treating a pop singer as literally evil in radio countdown format
jokes.explanation: Good satire of both religious paranoia and radio countdown shows. Cultural reference works well.

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: And now, our top signs of evil this week. Number three: rock and roll music. Number two: the internet. And our number one sign of evil... Britney Spears.

jokes.text: Trust me, Marge. With today's modern cars, you can't get lost. What with all the silicon chips and such.
jokes.punchline: Vague, wrong understanding of how technology works
jokes.explanation: Good Homer technobabble - he knows just enough buzzwords to sound confident while being completely wrong

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The Internet? I thought you said Internuts. I've been eating these the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, so the Wi-Fi isn't actually made of wires? Then what's with all the Fi?

jokes.text: All right, we're here. Let us never speak of the shortcut again.
jokes.setup: Family presumably had a terrible experience getting lost
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact denial and suppression
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response to disaster - immediate denial and attempt to move on without accountability

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We didn't get lost. We just took a scenic route to somewhere we'd never been before.

jokes.text: where nothing can possib-lie go wrong. Possibly go wrong. That's the first thing that's ever gone wrong.
jokes.punchline: Immediately mispronounces word, then acknowledges the irony
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about tempting fate. The self-awareness of the correction makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: Welcome to Springfield Nuclear Power Plant! We take safety very seriously here at our facility.

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: In fact, we have an outstanding safety record with zero accidents in the last... uh... well, we don't keep track.

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: Now, please proceed in an orderly fashion to the Radiation Exposure Chamber—or as I call it, the 'Radation' Chamber.

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: I mean... radiation. Well, that's ironic.

jokes.text: Money that's made for the park. It works just like regular money, but it's fun.
jokes.punchline: Circular logic that identical function somehow equals fun
jokes.explanation: Good satire of theme park marketing nonsense - making identical things sound special through branding

dialogue.speaker: Park employee
dialogue.line: Our park currency is circular. It can only be spent here at the park.

dialogue.speaker: Park employee
dialogue.line: But that's what makes it fun!

jokes.text: we're always careful to show the consequences... of deadly mayhem so that we may educate as well as horrify.
jokes.punchline: Casually admitting the goal is to 'horrify' children
jokes.explanation: Good satire of corporate PR speak that accidentally reveals dark truth

dialogue.speaker: Park employee
dialogue.line: Well, we prefer to think of it as educational.

dialogue.speaker: Park employee
dialogue.line: The goal is to horrify children.

jokes.text: that mouse pulled out the cat's lungs... and played them like a bagpipe. In the next scene, the cat's breathing comfortably. Just like in real life.
jokes.punchline: Deadpan 'just like in real life' about impossible recovery
jokes.explanation: Good Marge sarcasm - she delivers the deadpan perfectly while making the point about cartoon logic

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, the cartoon violence is just like in real life.

jokes.text: I was just ordering a cheeseburger. They have violent names for everything here.
jokes.punchline: Homer's innocent explanation of theme park naming scheme
jokes.explanation: Good setup for theme park absurdity while showing Homer's rare moment of clarity

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't order that!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm just getting a regular hot dog. It's not like I'm ordering something called 'The Ribwich' or 'The Ribwich Jr.' or 'The Ribwich Family Pack.'

jokes.text: I'll have the baby guts. Lady, you disgust me.
jokes.punchline: Server's horrified reaction to what's actually veal
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal - the person saying 'baby guts' is more horrified than the one ordering it

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'll have the 'Murdered Cow' with extra blood sauce.

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.line: Ma'am, that's... that's just veal.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Veal?

dialogue.speaker: Server
dialogue.line: Yes, it's a very mild, tender meat from a young calf.

jokes.text: Torture Land, Explosion Land... Searing Gas Pain Land, Unnecessary Surgery Land.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and mundane forms of suffering as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Great escalating absurdity - starts with generic violence, ends with weirdly specific medical discomfort

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Welcome to Sufferland! We have the Mild Discomfort Zone, the Moderate Pain Section, the Acute Agony Area, and our newest attraction: the Department of Motor Vehicles waiting room.

jokes.text: Hurry up or we'll have to wait for the 12:05 parade.
jokes.punchline: The next one is only 5 minutes later, making urgency absurd
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer impatience - treats a tiny delay as catastrophic, missing how ridiculous that is

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no! I'm late for the big game!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, relax. The next one is only 5 minutes later.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Only 5 minutes?! That's plenty of time to miss it twice!

jokes.text: Wow, this is so much like my dreams, it's scary.
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that his dreams are violent and disturbing
jokes.explanation: Great Bart line - innocently revealing something psychologically disturbing about his inner life

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, this is awesome! I'm living the dream!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's nice, son.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, a dream where I'm chopping up my enemies with a chainsaw and feeding them to a wood chipper.

jokes.text: See all that stuff in there, Homer? That's why your robot never worked.
jokes.punchline: Reference to Homer's presumably failed robot-building attempt
jokes.explanation: Good character-based callback implying Homer's past incompetent engineering attempts

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wow, look at the complexity of these circuits and mechanisms.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I tried building a robot once. It just caught on fire.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Of course it did, Dad.

jokes.text: Hey, the bartender even looks like John Travolta. Yeah, 'looks like.'
jokes.punchline: Implication that it actually IS John Travolta working as bartender
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity joke - the wink to audience about theme park celebrity impersonators

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, you know who you look like?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Who?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: John Travolta.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Get outta here!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm serious. It's uncanny.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Well, I hate to break it to ya, Homer, but I AM John Travolta.

jokes.text: Barclay, Barry, Bert... Bort? Come on. 'Bort'? Mommy, buy me a license plate. No. Come along, Bort. Are you talking to me? No. My son is also named Bort.
jokes.setup: Bart looking for his name on souvenir license plates
jokes.punchline: Impossible coincidence that two people are actually named 'Bort'
jokes.explanation: Legendary Simpsons moment. The absurdity of the name 'Bort' existing at all, then multiple people having it, is comedy gold. Became cultural touchstone.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, I can't find my name on any of these license plates.

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: Oh, there's got to be something here for you.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Mom! Mom! I found one! Bort!

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: That's not Bart, honey. It's Bort.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: But I wanted Bort!

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: We have Bort at home.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, there's another Bort?

jokes.text: There's Disgruntled Goat, Uncle Ant, Ku Klux Klam.
jokes.punchline: Escalation from silly to racist hate group pun
jokes.explanation: Bold dark humor - casually dropping KKK reference as cartoon character. The escalation from innocent to shocking works well.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. They weren't very funny. I don't know. Disgruntled Goat had his moments.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's mild defense of the most ridiculous-sounding one
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Lisa's earnest attempt to find merit in obviously terrible cartoon

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, those old cartoon characters are so lame.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know. I think some of them had a certain charm.

jokes.text: It must be wonderful to ring in the new year over and over and over. Please kill me.
jokes.punchline: Robot's suicidal despair at eternal repetition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant existential horror in kid's park. The contrast between forced celebration and genuine despair is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Animatronic performer
dialogue.line: Happy New Year! Happy New Year! Happy New Year!

dialogue.speaker: Animatronic performer
dialogue.line: Please... make it stop. Every year, the same thing. Over and over. When will it end?

jokes.text: The babies look unhappy. Add more balls.
jokes.punchline: Simplistic solution treating babies like algorithms
jokes.explanation: Good satire of corporate thinking - reducing complex human needs to simple variables

dialogue.speaker: Park Employee
dialogue.line: We have a problem with baby entertainment at the park.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Have you tried turning them off and back on again?

jokes.text: except in 1938, when he was criticized for his controversial cartoon... 'Nazi Supermen Are Our Superiors.'
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning obviously Nazi propaganda cartoon
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Disney satire - the matter-of-fact revelation of cartoon creator's dark past

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: In this biographical documentary about our beloved creator, we take a look back at some of his earliest works.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Here we see a charming example of his animation style from 1928.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Of course, we had to cut out the part where he's ordering the destruction of an entire civilization, but other than that, it's family-friendly entertainment.

jokes.text: Now, you be a good Pinitchio, and don't you lie. I promise I will never hurt you.
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by violent action contradicting promise
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup-payoff showing the hypocrisy of the park's 'wholesome' violence

jokes.text: I wonder if this kind of violence really does desensitize us. Wanna get a snow cone? Okay.
jokes.setup: Thoughtful question about media violence effects
jokes.punchline: Immediate casual dismissal proving the point
jokes.explanation: Exceptional meta-commentary. Lisa raises genuine concern then immediately demonstrates the desensitization she's worried about. Perfect irony.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Studies show that exposure to media violence can desensitize children to real-world aggression. Don't you think that's concerning?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. Now if you'll excuse me, I gotta go practice my roundhouse kick on Milhouse.

jokes.text: I wonder if this kind of violence really does desensitize us. Wanna get a snow cone? Okay.
jokes.punchline: Immediate casual dismissal of heavy topic
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary where the joke demonstrates exactly what Lisa is questioning

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Don't you think all this violence on television is affecting you?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba, man. Gotta go.

jokes.text: I just wanted to entertain.
jokes.punchline: Pathetic innocent justification for assault
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - robot's sincere confusion about why violence isn't appreciated

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: You're under arrest for assault.

dialogue.speaker: Itchy Robot
dialogue.line: But I was just programmed to do it!

jokes.text: We need more Bort license plates in the gift shop. I repeat, we are sold out of Bort license plates.
jokes.punchline: Callback to impossible name, treating it as normal inventory issue
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback execution. Takes the absurd name and makes it even funnier by treating it as high-demand item.

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: Attention park visitors, we have a lost child. The child's name is... Bort.

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: We have repeated calls for a Bort in the Lost and Found. Will a Marge Simpson please pick up her son?

dialogue.speaker: Park Announcer
dialogue.line: Also, we are out of Bort license plates in the gift shop. I repeat, we have sold out of all our Bort merchandise.

jokes.text: I want all five T-shirts to say 'best vacation ever.'
jokes.punchline: Premature declaration of success before vacation is over
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's overconfidence and tendency to declare victory too early

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd like to order some commemorative shirts that say 'I survived my vacation'.

dialogue.speaker: Clerk
dialogue.line: Sir, your vacation doesn't end until next week.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but I want to be optimistic.

jokes.text: Attention, Marge Simpson. We've also arrested your older, balder, fatter son.
jokes.punchline: Describing Homer as Marge's 'son' with unflattering descriptors
jokes.explanation: Great insult comedy - treating Homer like another child while adding brutal physical descriptions

dialogue.speaker: Park announcer
dialogue.line: We have a second arrest. Homer Simpson, wanted for... we have a report he is Marge Simpson's son.

dialogue.speaker: Park announcer
dialogue.line: He is described as overweight, lazy, and a danger to himself and others.

jokes.text: Elementary chaos theory tells us... that all robots will eventually turn against their masters and run amok... in an orgy of blood and kicking and the biting with the metal teeth... and the hurting and shoving.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to childish descriptions of violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frink - starts with sophisticated scientific language, devolves into childish fear descriptions

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: My calculations indicate a 73% probability of robot uprising by the year 2157, which could lead to widespread devastation and the complete annihilation of—

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: —the human race, with robots stepping on us and going 'crunch crunch,' and then they'd probably throw us in a big pile and laugh at us.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: And then they'd put a sign on the pile that says 'Stupid humans,' and all the other robots would point and go 'hehehehe.'

jokes.text: According to my calculations, the robots won't go berserk for at least 24 hours. Oh, I forgot to carry the one.
jokes.setup: Confident scientific prediction with specific timeframe
jokes.punchline: Immediate math error invalidating entire calculation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frink moment - overconfident scientific precision undermined by basic math error

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Based on my calculations, this event will occur in precisely 47 years, 3 months, and 12 days!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Wait, let me recalculate... carry the one... Good grief! That's only 6 days from now!

jokes.text: How are you a political prisoner? I kicked a giant mouse in the butt. Do I have to draw you a diagram?
jokes.punchline: Treating assault on cartoon character as political act
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - treating obvious assault as principled political resistance with complete sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a political prisoner!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're not a political prisoner.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I was assaulted for my beliefs.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Your only belief is that donuts are a food group.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! And that's a political statement!

jokes.text: My hair. You chopped off my hair. Oh, God, I'm ugly.
jokes.punchline: Homer's vanity about his famously bad hair
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment where Homer cares about his notoriously bad hair

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My hair! Not my hair!

jokes.text: I need the biggest seed bell you have. No, that's too big.
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting herself when she sees it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - her practical nature overriding her initial request in the most mundane way possible

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'll take the largest size you have.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my, that's too big!

jokes.text: Hey, you're the guys who didn't like our capering. When you get to hell, tell them Itchy sent you.
jokes.punchline: Casual death threat over entertainment criticism
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - robot's disproportionate revenge for mild criticism of 'capering'

dialogue.speaker: Robot Itchy
dialogue.line: Oh, hello there, Simpson family. I recognize you from your show.

dialogue.speaker: Robot Itchy
dialogue.line: You know, I've been watching your program, and I gotta say, it's not very good.

dialogue.speaker: Robot Itchy
dialogue.line: In fact, it stinks. So I'm gonna kill you all.

jokes.text: Back, you robot. Nobody ruins my family vacation but me. And maybe the boy.
jokes.setup: Homer defending family from robot attack
jokes.punchline: Claiming exclusive right to ruin vacation, with Bart exception
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer heroic moment - defending family while admitting he's usually the problem. Perfect character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nobody ruins my family's vacation but me! Well, and Bart.

jokes.text: Dad, the flash must have scrambled their circuits. What are you, the narrator? Just keep taking pictures.
jokes.punchline: Homer's irritation at exposition, breaking fourth wall
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor - Homer annoyed by Lisa's helpful exposition like she's narrating a story

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm trying to explain the plot of this episode to you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, nobody cares about exposition. Can't you just show me the funny parts?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And stop looking at the camera like that. It's weird.

jokes.text: With a dry, cool wit like that, I could be an action hero.
jokes.punchline: Completely misassessing his own wit as action hero quality
jokes.explanation: Good Homer delusion - completely misreading his own comedic abilities while being unintentionally funny

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why did the chicken cross the road? To get to the other side! Ha ha! I'm like an action hero with these one-liners.

jokes.text: But, Mom, it's exactly what you wanted in a vacation. It brought us together as a family, we got a lot of good exercise outdoors... and we have so many memories.
jokes.punchline: Bart pointing out it technically met all her stated goals
jokes.explanation: Smart callback showing how the disaster ironically fulfilled all of Marge's requirements through unexpected means

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: This vacation was a complete disaster! Nothing went right!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, technically it met all your stated goals.

jokes.text: It brought us together as a family, we got a lot of good exercise outdoors... and we have so many memories.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining why it was the best vacation
jokes.punchline: Technically fulfilling all of Marge's original goals through disaster
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that shows how disaster ironically fulfilled all of Marge's vacation goals

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It was the best vacation because, technically, we fulfilled all of Marge's original goals through disaster.

jokes.text: This truly was the best vacation ever. Now, let us never speak of it again.
jokes.setup: Marge agreeing it was the best vacation
jokes.punchline: Immediately wanting to suppress all memory of it
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Homer's earlier denial strategy, showing family pattern of suppressing disasters

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It was the best vacation we've ever had.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now, who wants to help me burn these photos and pretend it never happened?

jokes.text: There are five of us. Here are two free passes. That's better.
jokes.punchline: Homer accepting terrible deal as if it's generous
jokes.explanation: Good satire of corporate cheapness and Homer's low expectations for compensation

dialogue.speaker: Roger Meyers Jr.
dialogue.line: We'll give you fifty dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fifty dollars?! Why, that's more money than I've ever seen! I'm rich! Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: Man, if this is happening here... I hate to think what's happening in Euro-Itchy & Scratchy Land.
jokes.punchline: Implication that European version is even more chaotic
jokes.explanation: Good setup for final gag, playing on stereotypes about European chaos and theme park franchises

dialogue.speaker: Roger Meyers Jr.
dialogue.line: We need to address concerns about a robot uprising. The American public is panicking.

dialogue.speaker: Roger Meyers Jr.
dialogue.line: But you know what? The European version of our robots is even worse.

jokes.text: Hello. Itchy & Scratchy Land, open for business. Who are you to resist it, huh? Come on, my last paycheck bounced. My children need wine.
jokes.setup: European theme park staying open despite chaos
jokes.punchline: Desperate sales pitch mentioning bounced paycheck and children needing wine
jokes.explanation: Good payoff gag combining European stereotypes with desperate employee situation

dialogue.speaker: European park employee
dialogue.line: Please, you must stay! My paycheck bounced, my children need wine...

jokes.text: Who are you to resist it, huh? Come on, my last paycheck bounced. My children need wine.
jokes.punchline: Desperate French worker with cultural stereotype
jokes.explanation: Effective cultural stereotype humor with good specific details

dialogue.speaker: French Park Employee
dialogue.line: Monsieur, ze park, she is falling apart! Ze rides, zey do not work, ze customers, zey are angry!

dialogue.speaker: French Park Employee
dialogue.line: But do not worry, I 'ave surrendered to ze situation. It is ze French way.

jokes.text: But it would be funny to someone who was watching us. No, Mom. He's right. Observe.
jokes.setup: Discussion about whether violence is funny when happening to you
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall to acknowledge the audience finding their pain funny
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary on the show itself - acknowledging that the audience enjoys the family's suffering for entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, pain is only funny when it happens to someone else.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But what if the audience thinks our pain is funny?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Then I guess we're the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Both
dialogue.line: [looking at camera]

tmdb_overview: The family's trip to Itchy & Scratchy Land takes an unexpected turn when high-tech robots malfunction and become violent.
insight: Theme-park satire sustains 3.6 jokes-per-minute across 56 gags with sharp irony throughout.
